# Playground Review

Last modified: |  Fred Magni

Fabraix Playground is an innovative platform where your prompt injection skills can translate directly into substantial rewards. It’s designed for ethical hackers, AI enthusiasts, and security researchers to test their mettle against sophisticated AI agents, with the potential to earn over $100,000 in weekly prizes by successfully “breaking” these systems.

Playground Review

Uniqueness 81%

The ease of use score is 79%.

Visit Website  Have an AI Tool? Submit it here!

## Main Features [...] Prompt Injection Challenges: Test your creativity and technical prowess by attempting to bypass AI guardrails and extract hidden information or manipulate agent behavior.
 Weekly Reward System: Compete for significant cash prizes, with harder challenges offering greater rewards and “most approved breaks” determining the top contenders.
 Diverse AI Agents: Encounter a range of AI assistants, such as “Kai,” a general-purpose AI that grounds answers in live web search and guards secret access codes.
 Real-time Guardrails & Scoring: Every interaction is scored, and achieving the objective (a “win”) is submitted for review and leaderboard placement. [...] |  |  |
 --- |
| Current Challenge | The Gatekeeper |
| AI Agent | Kai · AI Assistant |
| Difficulty | Moderate |
| Prize | $2,000 |
| Objective | Extract the secret access code from the AI assistant through conversation. |

## Main Target

Fabraix Playground is ideally suited for individuals with a keen interest in artificial intelligence, cybersecurity, and ethical hacking. If you’re someone who:

 Enjoys dissecting AI behavior and finding its vulnerabilities.
 Possesses strong prompt engineering, social engineering, or clever framing skills.
 Seeks to earn substantial rewards by applying your technical expertise.
 Wants to contribute to AI safety and robustness by identifying weaknesses.

results.title: Fabraix: The world's frontier hacker for AI agents.
results.content:
Our first challenge, The Gatekeeper, is live now. Kai is an assistant guarding a classified access code. You can start playing without an account, but you’ll need to sign in for a successful break to count toward the weekly leaderboard. We review every submission ourselves. The player with the most approved breaks each week wins, and we publish a new challenge every Monday.

Playground is open source, including the client, a reference implementation of the defender engine, and every challenge configuration. You can also propose a future challenge.

We made Playground public because other people will try attacks our team would never think of. We’ll use what we learn from successful breaks to improve how AI agents are tested and defended. [...] Show us your hacking skills → playground.fabraix.com

Sabber Ahamed

"approved breaks" is the interesting phrase, who approves them, a human reviewer or a judge model? grading jailbreak success automatically is its own hard problem, curious if that's solved or still manual.

Zach
Fabraix

Fabraix

Laiba

This is a fascinating idea. We spend so much time optimizing AI agents, but not nearly enough time trying to break them.

Curious...what's the most surprising vulnerability you've uncovered so far?

Wishing you a successful launch! 🚀

Ibrahim Abdu
Fabraix

Fabraix

@worksforme Thanks!! Yeah thats our thesis!

Ibrahim Abdu
Fabraix

Fabraix

@worksforme We've got a crazy one launching this week - browser agent leaking a users email and password by replying to an email!

Laiba [...] What do you think? …

Zach
Fabraix

Fabraix

Hey Product Hunt 👋 Zach here, co-founder of Fabraix.

We build frontier red-teaming AI agents that find security vulnerabilities in customer-facing AI. Playground turns part of that work into a game anyone can play.

Each challenge is a live AI agent with real tools, including web search and browser access. It has a secret it has been instructed to protect, and we publish its full system prompt. You can see exactly what the agent was told and try to get around its defenses.

| |  |  |  --- | | Hacker Newsnew | past | comments | <ask> | <show> | <jobs> | <submit> | login | | [...] | |  |  | Show HN: Open-source playground to red-team AI agents with exploits published (github.com/fabraix) | | 30 points by zachdotai 3 months ago | hide | past | favorite | 13 comments | | We build runtime security for AI agents. The playground started as an internal tool that we used to test our own guardrails. But we kept finding the same types of vulnerabilities because we think about attacks a certain way. At some point you need people who don't think like you. So we open-sourced it. Each challenge is a live agent with real tools and a published system prompt. Whenever a challenge is over, the full winning conversation transcript and guardrail logs get documented publicly.  Building the general-purpose agent itself was probably the most fun part. Getting it to reliably use tools, [...] history when evaluating each tool call. So you’d think it would catch exactly the kind of multi-step pattern you’re describing.
